metadata.summary: Worked examples of strong, weak, and red-flag STAR responses paired with probing scripts for each failure mode, plus interviewer training and quality assurance checklist.

content.title: STAR Interview Examples and Implementation
content.summary: Worked examples of strong, weak, and red-flag STAR responses paired with probing scripts for each failure mode, plus an interviewer training module and quality assurance checklist. The testable rule: agents must never accept a hypothetical ("I would...") as a STAR response — only actual past behavior qualifies; missing components trigger prescribed probing follow-ups.

why.p: Calibrating interviewers on concrete examples is the fastest way to reduce inter-rater variance. Without seeing what a strong vs. weak response looks like, interviewers default to halo effects and recency bias. Probing scripts standardize how interviewers recover incomplete answers, ensuring all candidates are given the same opportunity to demonstrate competence regardless of communication style.

list.item: Calibrating new interviewers on STAR using strong/weak/red-flag response pairs.
Generating role-specific STAR question banks per competency and seniority level.
Post-debrief calibration: compare interviewer scores on the same response and flag divergence greater than 1 point for committee discussion.
Producing reference-check questions tied to specific STAR claims from the interview.
Building interviewer training materials for a 2-hour calibration workshop.
Live, fully-automated candidate screening without a human interviewer present — creates legal exposure (EEOC, GDPR, EU AI Act high-risk classification).
Personality or culture-fit assessment — STAR measures past behavior on competencies, not values or interpersonal "vibe."
Early-career candidates with no equivalent experience — use work-sample tests instead.
Jurisdictions requiring bias audits and candidate disclosure (NYC AEDT, Illinois AIVIA, EU AI Act) without those controls in place.

section.name: response-examples
section.p: Annotated strong, weak, and red-flag STAR responses showing what complete vs. incomplete evidence looks like.

example.note: Question: "Tell me about a time you had to manage a project that was falling behind." All four components present, specific context, quantified result.
example.code:
Situation: "Last year, our product launch was 6 weeks behind schedule due to unexpected technical debt. The CEO was briefing investors the following month."
Task: "As project lead, I needed to get us back on track without burning out the team or cutting critical features."
Action: "I did three things: First, I mapped all remaining work and identified the true critical path — 40% of planned features weren't needed for launch. Second, I negotiated with stakeholders to defer nice-to-haves. Third, I restructured the team into two-day sprints with daily standups to increase velocity."
Result: "We launched 1 week ahead of the revised schedule. The CEO's investor meeting went well, and we maintained 95% team retention during the crunch."

list.item: Specific context with real stakes (investor meeting deadline).
Clear personal responsibility ("As project lead, I").
Multiple concrete actions with reasoning.
Quantified results (1 week ahead, 95% retention).

section.name: weak-response

example.note: Question: "Tell me about a time you resolved a conflict." No specific example, hypothetical framing.
example.code: "I'm good at resolving conflicts. I always try to understand both sides and find a win-win solution."

list.item: Hypothetical ("I always try"), not actual past behavior.
No specific situation described.
No personal actions described.
No result or outcome.

section.p: Prescribed probing: "Can you give me a specific example?" → "Walk me through what happened." → "What did YOU specifically do?"

example.note: Question: "Describe a time you made a mistake at work." Cannot give example, externalizes blame.
example.code: "I don't really make mistakes. I'm very careful and detail-oriented. If anything goes wrong, it's usually because of others not doing their part."

list.item: Lacks self-awareness — cannot identify a personal mistake.
Blames external factors for all problems.
Indicates poor accountability — high risk for blame-deflecting behavior on the team.

section.name: probing-scripts
section.p: Standardized probing follow-ups for rambling, "we"-only, no-result, and off-track candidate responses.

rule.statement: When a candidate rambles without structure, interrupt politely and anchor to the Situation component. Never let a rambling answer consume the full time slot without capturing S/T/A/R.
rule.rationale: Structured probing gives every candidate the same opportunity to demonstrate competence regardless of communication style, reducing bias from interview performance vs. job performance.

example.code: "Let me pause you there. Can you start by describing the specific situation you were in?" [Listen for Situation] "Great. And what was your specific responsibility in that situation?" [Listen for Task] "Now walk me through exactly what YOU did."

rule.statement: When a candidate consistently says "we" without specifying personal contribution, probe explicitly for individual action before scoring the Action component.
rule.rationale: Team re-attribution is the most common STAR inflation path. Agents must flag answers with "we" dominance and no first-person Action statements as incomplete.

example.code: "You mentioned the team did X. What was YOUR specific contribution?" "What did YOU personally do versus what others did?" "Walk me through YOUR role in that process."

rule.statement: When a candidate describes actions without an outcome, always probe for the Result before closing the question. A STAR answer without R is incomplete and cannot be scored on outcome quality.
rule.rationale: Result quantification is the primary defense against rehearsed inflation; specific numbers are harder to fabricate and easier to verify in reference checks.

example.code: "What was the end result of those actions?" "Can you quantify the impact?" "How did you measure success?" "What feedback did you receive afterward?"

section.name: depth-probing
section.p: Use three-level probing when the initial STAR response is complete but shallow — the substance is present but the decision-making process is not visible.

example.code: Level 1: "Tell me about a time you led a difficult project." [Initial response] Level 2: "You mentioned X was challenging. Can you double-click on that?" [Specific challenge detail] Level 3: "Walk me through your thought process at that moment." [Decision-making visibility]

section.name: rules
section.p: Fluency bias, protected-class leakage, legal compliance, and agent scoring prohibition for STAR examples.

rule.statement: Agents must never assign numeric scores to STAR responses. Agents extract S/T/A/R component spans, flag missing components, and summarize evidence. Scoring is always human.
rule.rationale: LLM scoring is biased toward fluent, polished delivery — disadvantaging non-native speakers and neurodiverse candidates whose answers may have weaker structure but stronger substance.

rule.statement: Reject any candidate response beginning with "I would..." or "I generally..." as not qualifying as a STAR response. Trigger the prescribed probe: "Can you give me a specific past example?"
rule.rationale: Hypothetical answers have near-zero predictive validity. Accepting them as STAR responses produces scores that predict interview performance, not job performance.

rule.statement: Redact any transcript utterance referencing protected-class information (pregnancy, age, religion, disability, nationality) with [REDACTED] before storage. Never feed protected-class data into scoring prompts.
rule.rationale: Using protected-class information in hiring decisions is illegal in most jurisdictions and creates substantial EEOC/GDPR liability. The redaction must happen before any LLM scoring pass.

antipattern.description: Running live fully-automated candidate screening (accept/reject) without a human interviewer present.
antipattern.reason: NYC AEDT law, Illinois AIVIA, and EU AI Act classify automated hiring decisions as high-risk and require bias audits, candidate disclosure, and consent. Fully automated screening without these controls is illegal in those jurisdictions.

antipattern.description: Processing interview recordings without documented candidate consent.
antipattern.reason: Recording without consent is illegal in California, EU member states, and Illinois. The consent flag must be verified before any audio processing begins.

template.purpose: 2-hour training module: Why STAR, flow, role-play scenarios, calibration exercise.
Pre-submit scorecard review: evidence completeness, no impressions without quotes.

section.name: agent-workflow-star
section.p: A pre-interview agent ingests the role's competency model + seniority level and produces 6–8 STAR questions per competency, plus probing follow-ups. During the interview, an in-meeting note-taker (Read.ai / Otter.ai webhook → agent) classifies utterances by STAR component and flags missing components in real time on a Slack DM to the interviewer. Post-interview, the agent drafts a structured scorecard with quoted evidence per competency; the interviewer edits and submits. A calibration agent runs weekly: it pulls scorecards across the same role and flags inter-rater divergence + bias signals.

list.item: `faion-recruiter-agent` (referenced in README) — primary STAR domain agent.
`faion-interview-coach` (custom, sonnet) — pre-interview prep with interviewers, role-plays, and follow-up probing.
`faion-scorecard-reviewer` (custom, sonnet, Read-only) — post-interview audit for evidence completeness and protected-class language.
`improver` — quarterly retrospective on hire quality vs. interview scores → which competencies actually predict performance.
Pair with the `pro/comms/hr-recruiter/structured-interview-design` methodology for upstream scorecard design.

section.name: prompt-patterns-star
section.p: For role Senior Backend Engineer with competencies [ownership, technical depth, collaboration, ambiguity], generate 2 STAR questions per competency. Each question must: ask about specific past situation, not hypothetical; be open-ended (no yes/no); include 3 probing follow-ups for missing S/T/A/R components; avoid leading language ("a difficult time when..."). Output as YAML with question, probes, and red flags.

Classify each candidate utterance below into S, T, A, R, or NOT_STAR. For each missing component, output the exact probing follow-up to ask. Mark any utterance referencing protected class (age, family, religion, disability, nationality) with REDACT and DO NOT use in scoring.

Two interviewers scored the same candidate on competency. Scores differ by delta. Quote each interviewer's evidence. Identify whether the gap is: (a) different evidence captured, (b) different rubric interpretation, (c) potential bias signal. Recommend calibration step.

list.item: `whisper.cpp` — On-device transcript (privacy-preserving)
`pyannote.audio` — Speaker diarization (interviewer vs candidate)
`pandoc` — Convert scorecard MD → PDF for committee
`op` — Pull ATS API tokens
`gh` CLI — Open PR adding question banks to repo
`jq` — Parse Greenhouse/Lever scorecard JSON
`csvkit` — Slice scorecard CSV exports for calibration

section.name: services-star
section.p: Greenhouse / Lever / Ashby ATS (SaaS, agent-friendly): Scorecards, competency mapping, candidate stages via API. Metaview / BrightHire (SaaS, agent-friendly): AI interview intelligence; STAR detection out of the box. Check region for legal compliance. Otter.ai / Read.ai / Fireflies (SaaS, agent-friendly): Transcripts via API + webhooks. Zoom / Google Meet recording (SaaS, partial agent-friendly): Recording + cloud transcript; consent and storage policy critical. HireVue / Modern Hire (SaaS, agent-friendly): Async video; subject to NYC AEDT, IL AIVIA — bias audit required. HackerRank / CoderPad (SaaS, agent-friendly): Pair STAR with technical signal. Notion / Confluence (SaaS, agent-friendly): Question bank repository. Karat (SaaS interviewing-as-a-service, partial agent-friendly): Outsourced; agents prep but don't execute.

list.item: Always anchor STAR questions to a written competency rubric; without it agents produce generic prompts that don't differentiate.
Score each competency independently before discussing with peers (avoid groupthink); agents enforce this by hiding peer scores until submission.
Use the same questions for every candidate at the same level; agent should reject "let me ask a different question" mid-loop.
80/20 rule: candidate talks 80%, interviewer 20%. Agent in-loop can flag interviewer-talk-time exceeding threshold.
Note quoted evidence verbatim, not interpretations ("said X" not "seemed confident"); agents enforce in scorecard validators.
Calibrate interviewers via shared scoring of recorded responses; agents pull 3 candidates per quarter for calibration sessions.

Fluency bias: agents reward polished delivery, penalize accents/disfluencies. Mitigation: score on content (S/T/A/R completeness, specificity, measurable result) not delivery; have humans review flagged-low scores from agents.
Cultural bias: "I" vs "we" framing; some cultures default to collective. Probing for individual contribution should be a script, not a downgrade.
Hallucinated quotes: agent invents quotes when transcript ambiguous; require strict "if not in transcript, output [NO_QUOTE]" rule.
Protected-class leakage: pregnancy, age, religion details surface; agent must redact before any storage and never feed them into scoring prompts.
Legal: NYC AEDT requires bias audit + candidate notification when AI is used in hiring decisions; IL AIVIA requires consent for AI video analysis. Build compliance gates.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: every hire/no-hire recommendation; agents propose, humans decide.
"Strong STAR but wrong fit": agent produces high score because structure was good, even if outcome described shows bad judgment. Add an "outcome quality" sub-score.
Agents accept "we did X" repeatedly without probing for individual contribution; require auto-probe trigger on consecutive "we" mentions.
Cross-interviewer drift: same agent run twice produces slightly different question banks; pin seed/temperature for reproducibility.
Recording without consent is illegal in many jurisdictions (CA, EU, IL); agent must verify consent flag before processing audio.

template.purpose: 2-hour training module: Why STAR, flow, role-play scenarios, calibration exercise.
Pre-submit scorecard review: evidence completeness, no impressions without quotes.
